what_it_does: Creates a static HTML component bench page showing six control treatments: gold primary action states, quiet secondary/destructive actions, dial controls, search with segmented options, a glass rail, and hotspots.
observations: Uses absolute positioning for a six-cell grid inside a framed view.
Defines all page-specific layout and component styling in an inline style block.
Loads shared piece and phone stylesheets plus Google fonts.
Uses CSS variables throughout for color, spacing, radius, and type scale.
Injects icons at runtime with DNA.org.icon from /surface/runtime/organisms.js.
Represents controls as visual div/span elements rather than form controls or buttons.
standouts: Six named cells organize controls as specimen-like examples.
Gold is used only on the primary action, slider knobs, selected segment, and active rail icon.
Destructive action is rendered as a quiet outline treatment rather than a filled warning field.
Glass rail and hotspots demonstrate controls over an image-like gradient surface.
conventions: Follows the page shell convention with .view, .title, .mark, and .foot elements.
Uses design-token CSS variables instead of hard-coded spacing and type values in most declarations.
Uses numbered icon placeholders i1 through i6 populated by a small script.
Mixes reusable classes with inline styles for one-off positioning and state examples.
concerns: The mock controls are non-semantic div/span elements, so they do not behave as accessible interactive controls.
Several widths, positions, and heights are fixed, which may constrain responsive behavior without the linked phone stylesheet.
The page depends on DNA.org.icon and shared CSS variables being available at runtime.
Inline styles in the markup make some specimen states harder to reuse or audit.
notes: A static visual ledger page for documenting how control treatments should look and feel in this design language.
questions: Are these controls intended only as visual specimens, or should any become interactive components?
Are the inline state examples expected to be promoted into reusable classes?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated component bench controls purpose matches the actual content: the file presents a visual catalog of control styles and states.
apparent_themes: Secondary controls stay quiet and low-contrast.
Destructive intent is signaled with restraint.
Controls are framed as part of a design language rather than generic UI widgets.
Dark glass and hotspots imply interaction over visual or spatial surfaces.
intention_signals: It seems intended to codify control hierarchy, state language, and surface behavior for a design system through static visual specimens.
novelty: The file presents UI controls as named design specimens with descriptive microcopy, generated organism icons, and surface-specific treatments like glass rails and hotspots.
contribution: Contributes a compact file-local catalog of control treatments, hierarchy cues, state examples, and interaction-surface patterns that a fused system could retain as design-system reference material.
summary_for_embedding: Static HTML/CSS component bench page showing six styled control specimens for primary actions, secondary/destructive actions, dials, search/segments, glass rail navigation, and hotspots.
intention_for_embedding: This file was for documenting and demonstrating how controls in the Counterpart design language should express action, restraint, selection, and surface interaction.
